text: This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on circulating-fuel reactors and other ANP research at the Laboratory under its Contract W-7405-eng-26.
The report is divided into five major parts: 1.
Aircraft Reactor Engineering, 2.
Radiation Damage and Fuel Recovery and Reprocessing, and 5.
The ART is to be a power plant system that will include a 60-Mw circulating-fuel reflector-moderator reactor and adequate means for heat disposal.

The results of this study indicate that the calculated maximum stress is quickly reduced to about $80\%$ of the predicted elastic value and remains essentially constant for the life of the member.

Series of tests and experiments are under way in order to determine the fatigue life of Inconel under simulated ART conditions, since the core shells might be subjected to surface temperature oscillations as large as $80^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at frequencies of from $\frac{1}{4}$ to 4 cps.

Flow studies of the full-scale 21-in. plastic model of the ART core were continued in order to investigate wall temperature fluctuations, which may be analogous to the fluid temperature fluctuations observed in the one-half-scale volume-heat-source experiment.

Some improvements in flow distribution, both radially and circumferentially, were obtained with an inlet guide vane, a conical baffle plate, and a slotted disk, which was located 6 in. above the equator.
(LTSF) experiments showed that the dose varied exponentially with lead thickness up to about 4.5 in.; the ART lead is 4.3 in. thick.
The LTSF data are interpreted to mean that the intensity of gamma rays penetrating the 4.3-in.-thick lead shield of the ART will be much greater than the intensity of gamma rays produced in the lead.
The calculated dose rate at the surface of the shield was found to be 1840 r/hr.

The relative importance of each source is indicated by the percentage contribution to the total dose rate: core, $22\%$ ; outer core shell, $9\%$ ; reflector, $12\%$ ; reflector shell, $35\%$ ; heat exchanger, $22\%$ .

It was found that at a distance of 50 ft from the center of the ART operating at 60 Mw with a 4.3-in.-lead and 31.5-in.-water shield, the calculated gamma-ray dose is 22.8 rem/hr and the fast-neutron dose rate is 0.05 rem/hr.

Tests of commercially available resistance-type liquid-metal-level transducers were continued, and when the tests were terminated at 3732 hr, during which time the NaK level of the system was cycled 65,000 times, only one of the four instruments was in operating condition.

All-welded resistance-type on-off level probes are being developed as replacements for conventional spark-plug probes, which are not suitable for pressurized systems and temperatures above $450^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
An especially fabricated spark plug with a beryllium oxide insulator was tested in NaK as an on-off level probe, but it failed in 20 hr of operation because of short circuiting of the insulator.
It is hoped that the present average life of 1100 hr in a static-level system can be increased to the specified 3000 hr.
Also a second set of pneumatic-output transmitters completed a successful 3000-hr life test in a NaK-filled test loop.
A 1-in. turbine flowmeter ceased to operate after 340 hr of an endurance test scheduled for 3000 hr in a circulating fused salt.
A $3\frac{1}{2}$ -in. turbine flowmeter operating in a NaK system failed some time during the first 40 hr of operation.
The Heliarc welding process has been found to yield 30 to $50\%$ acceptable welds.
Bend tests were made, and single U-bends on a $\frac{3}{4}$ -in. radius were found to have little effect on accuracy.
Repeated bending caused a 2 to $3^{\circ}$ shift.
If the irradiation tests show either UCON LB-140-X or Gulfcrest 34 lubricants to be satisfactory, buna-N-base O-rings will be specified for the reactor pump seals.
with NaF-ZrF4-UF4 (50-46-4 mole %, fuel 30) as the circulated fluid were started.
The pump head is 1 to 2 ft lower than that obtained with water, and this discrepancy is being investigated.

A similar pump that operated with fuel 30 in an endurance test was shut down after a total of 2587 hr of operating time and 120 thermal cycles in the temperature range of 1400 to $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

There was no trouble with the hydraulic drive during the last 1240 hr of operation because a back pressure was maintained on the discharge side of the motor to ensure that the piston shoes were seated on the wobble plate at all times.

An ART sodium pump is also being tested in an endurance test with sodium and has accumulated 1584 hr of operating time with 300 thermal cycles in the temperature range of 1250 to $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
High-temperature testing of an ART primary NaK pump was continued, and performance and efficiency curves were obtained on a NaK temperature of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
One test was terminated by a NaK leak in the circulating cold-trap economizer after a total of 1845 hr of operation.
The heat exchanger and the radiator in this system had been operated nonisothermally for 1456 hr, including 552 hr at a fuel inlet temperature of $1600^{\circ}F$ and 29 thermal cycles.
A 20-tube semicircular heat exchanger was designed and is being fabricated that simulates the tube geometries of the ART main heat exchangers.
With the improved model 32 impellers in the pumps, the liquid spraying and sloshing noted previously in the expansion tank and the resultant wetting of the tank ceiling were greatly reduced.
The model 32 impeller also resolved the problem of liquid rising in the shaft annulus of a stopped pump if the other pump was still running.
All tests to date have been made with fuel 30, but a test stand is now being modified for tests with NaK.
Traps packed with $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ have operated satisfactorily in several tests.

Much installation work on the ETU assembly has been done, and preliminary estimates show an expected ETU facility completion date, exclusive of the reactor and its associated equipment, of September 1, 1958.

A $\frac{1}{12}$ -scale model of a proposed hot cell is now available as an aid in visualizing the complex techniques that will be involved and in designing the tools and fixtures that will be required.
A study of manpower required for various phases of the program indicated that the peak work load during operation of the ART will require 40 engineers and 18 technicians.

A pump is now available that will be suitable for a loop with maximum temperatures in the range of 1600 to $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , which correspond to a pump temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The fluid friction characteristics on the fuel side of a straight-tube model of the ART fuel-to-NaK heat exchanger were tested further, with measurements being taken down to a Reynolds modulus of 150.
The enthalpy and heat capacity for the mixture NaF-ZrF4-UF4 (50-25-25 mole %) were determined in both the solid and liquid states.
The viscosities ranged from 7.5 to 9.5 centipoises at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ down to 3.3 to
3.5 centipoises at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
With the modified apparatus it has been possible to analyze samples containing up to $80\% \mathrm{ZrF}_4$ with negligible loss in weight of the sample.
Preliminary visual observations indicate that reasonably accurate melt temperatures can be measured with only $150\mathrm{mg}$ of material.
Quenching studies of $\mathsf{LiF - UF_4}$ systems that contain 20 to 32 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{UF}_4$ confirmed the liquidus and solidus relationships previously reported.
It was found that the compound $4\mathsf{LiF - UF}_4$ undergoes very slow subsolidus decomposition to LiF and $7\mathsf{LiF - UF}_4$ at about $470^{\circ}C$ .
Thermal analysis studies showed that these mixtures undercooled readily if the melt was first heated to over $800^{\circ}C$ .
In order to be sure that the quenching results characterized the equilibrium conditions, a seeding experiment was conducted on a mixture containing 24 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{UF}_4$ .
The results were in accordance with the results of the quenching studies, except that the temperatures of the thermal effects were about $10^{\circ}C$ lower.

For mixtures containing 33.3 and 40 mole % NoF, the ternary compound is the primary phase, the solidus temperature is $452^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and RbF-BeF $_2$ is the other phase below the solidus.
The investigation of the equilibrium reduction of $\mathsf{NiF}_2$ by $H_{2}$ in NaF-ZrF4 at $550^{\circ}C$ was concluded, and the data were combined with those previously obtained.
In a careful determination of the solubility of nickel as $\mathsf{NiF}_2$ in NaF-ZrF4 (53-47 mole %) at $550^{\circ}C$ , a value of $352 \pm 11$ ppm was obtained.
The data indicated that the solubility remained constant when the amount of saturating phase changed from a trace to a total of 1310 ppm $\mathsf{Ni}^{+ + }$ .
Further, the value of 352 is in substantial agreement with a value of 400 ppm previously reported for the solubility obtained with an addition of 1700 ppm $\mathsf{Ni}^{+ + }$ .
Additional experiments on the reduction of $UF_{4}$ by $Fe^{0}$ in the reaction medium $RbF-ZrF_{4}$ (60-40 mole %) showed that equilibrium was established
in 5 hr and that the equilibrium concentration of $\mathsf{Fe}^{+ + }$ is significantly higher at 600 than of $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Additional data on the reaction of UF with Cr in the RbF-ZrF reaction medium at 600 and at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ indicate that the fluoride ion activity increases only slightly when the RbF concentration is increased from 50 to 58 mole%.

Rare-earth fluorides labeled with Ce $^{141}$ , La $^{140}$ , and Sm $^{153}$ were used to study the solubilities of CeF $_3$ and LaF $_3$ alone and in combination and SmF $_3$ alone and in combination with CeF $_3$ in NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole %) at various temperatures.
The determination of the solubility of xenon gas in molten $\mathsf{NoF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (50-46-4 mole %) as a function of pressure and temperature was successfully completed.
A direct equilibration method was used for determining the solubility of HF in $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF}_4$ (53-47 mole %) at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The data obtained, which will be extended to higher temperatures and pressures, indicate that the solubility is $1.25 \times 10^{-5}$ moles of HF per cubic centimeter of melt at 1 atm.

Preliminary measurements of concentration cells with $\mathrm{Ni}^{\circ} / \mathrm{NiF}_2$ electrodes and the solvent KF-LiF (50-50 mole %) indicate the solubility of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ in this solvent to be about 10 times that in the solvent NaF-ZrF4 (53-47 mole %).

In order to obtain more information regarding the change of solute activity coefficients with solvent composition, efforts are under way to develop more reliable types of Daniell cells and to measure a series of these cells in different media with varying but dilute concentrations of the metal fluorides, $\mathsf{NiF}_{2^{\prime}}$ , $\mathsf{FeF}_{2^{\prime}}$ , $\mathsf{CrF}_{2^{\prime}}$ and possibly other solutes.

Some preliminary experiments with $\mathsf{Li}^{+}$ and $\mathsf{Ni}^{++}$ ions in a chloride melt showed that if the chloride lattice is considered to be stationary, the $\mathsf{Li}^{+}$ ions moved about 20 times faster than the $\mathsf{Ni}^{++}$ ions.
At $1075^{\circ}C$ , a density of $6.80 \pm 0.09~\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ was found by using graphite pycnometers.

The density of the molten material being greater than that of solid $UF_4$ ( $6.63~\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ based on x-ray data) and the behavior of the salt surface level upon freezing in the pycnometer indicate that $UF_4$ either contracts or expands only very slightly upon melting.
Considerable quantities of $\mathrm{YF}_3$ ( $\sim 1400\mathrm{g}$ ) were therefore prepared for use in manufacturing the metallic yttrium from which the hydride is prepared.

The product of the reduction of $\mathsf{MoF}_6$ with $\mathsf{FeF}_2$ was collected in a condenser and redistilled, and the yellow product was found to melt at $78^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ rather than the $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ previously reported for the material before distillation.
In the pilot-scale equipment, 42 batches were processed that totaled 1575 lb of various fluoride compositions, with a major portion of the material being NaF-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ (11.2-41-45.3-2.5 mole %).

Inconel cans are now being used for 50-lb batches, but when inadvertent exposure of the mixture to air or water occurs in Inconel cans, extensive contamination of the melt with $\mathrm{CrF}_2$ and $\mathrm{FeF}_2$ results.
In the production-scale facility 46 batches totaling approximately 11,500 lb were processed.

A sensitive, spectrophotometric method for the determination of nickel with 4-isopropyl-1, 2-cyclohexanedionedioxime was developed.
When the nickel-dioxime complex is extracted with xylene, the ultimate sensitivity is less than 0.5 ppm of nickel in a 5-g sample of alkali metal.

Three forced-circulation loops fabricated of Hastelloy B were examined that had circulated NaF-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ (11.2-41-45.3-2.5 mole %, fuel 107) at a maximum fluid temperature of $1650^{\circ}\text{F}$ .
Two of the loops operated with maximum wall temperatures of about $1750^{\circ}\text{F}$ and showed no evidence of mass transfer after 407 and 1000 hr, respectively.
A third loop, which operated with a maximum wall temperature of $1710^{\circ}\text{F}$ and at a relatively low flow rate, showed slight evidence of deposits near the pump.

An Inconel forced-circulation loop was operated with NaF-ZrF4-UF (50-46-4 mole %, fuel 30) at a maximum fluid temperature of about $1700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and a maximum wall temperature of $1840^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
After 1000 hr of operation the loop was found to have been attacked to a depth of 9 mils, and there were some deposits in cold-leg sections.
70, NaF-ZrF4-UF (56-39-5 mole %), was tested in another Inconel forced-circulation loop operated at a fluid temperature of about $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Loop operation was terminated after 716 hr, and attack to a depth of 5 mils was found in the hot leg.
An examination of another Inconel forced-circulation loop that operated 500 hr with fuel mixture No.
30 also revealed hot-leg attack to a depth of 5 mils, but no cold-leg deposits were found.

Cold traps maintained at temperatures of 100, 300, 600, and $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , respectively, were utilized in conjunction with loops operating at a maximum NaK temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and a fluid temperature drop through the loop of $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

A definite increase in the amount of deposit material was noted in the loop which contained the cold trap operated at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ as compared with the loop having the cold trap operated at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The change in the amount of deposited material accompanying a decrease in the cold-trap temperature below $600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was less than the change from 800 to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Two Inconel-sodium forced-circulation loops which operated at flow rates of 1.5 and 3.0 gpm were also examined.
The maximum sodium temperature in these tests was $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and the temperature gradient was $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

A type 304 stainless steel forced-circulation loop after operation with sodium at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ showed only slight traces of mass-transfer deposits.
This loop was operated for 1000 hr with a thermal gradient of $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Long duration tests of Inconel forced-circulation loops were made with both NaK and sodium circulated at a maximum fluid temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The test with NaK, for which a temperature gradient of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was used, was terminated after 2760 hr.
The cooled section of this loop was found to contain extensive crystalline deposits that ranged to 14 mils in thickness.
The loop operated with sodium for 4000 hr with a temperature gradient of $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ showed considerably larger deposits that reached a thickness of 50 mils in some areas.

Thermal-convection loops fabricated from various experimental nickel-molybdenum alloys were tested with sodium and with NaF-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ (11.2-41-45.3-2.5 mole %, fuel 107), for 1000 and 500 hr, respectively, at maximum fluid temperatures of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
There was attack by the fuel mixture to an average depth of 1.5 mils in all loops except those constructed of alloys containing aluminum additions.
For the alloys containing aluminum a maximum depth of attack of 3 mils was found.
An average hot-leg attack of 1 mil was found in several nickel-molybdenum alloy loops operated with sodium, and deposits were visible in the cold legs.
A series of standard Inconel thermal-convection loops was operated with NaF-ZrF4-UF4 (50-46-4 mole %, fuel 30) at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to determine whether

Three stainless-steel-clad niobium loops showed only slight hot-leg attack in tests with NaF-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ (11.2-4)-45.3-2.5 mole $\%$ , fuel 107) at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; however, some cold-leg deposits were observed in niobium loops which operated 1000 hr or longer.
A niobium loop was also operated 1000 hr with sodium at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Additional Inconel tube-to-header joints with recrystallized welds were corrosion tested in NaK (56.44 wt %) and in NaF-ZrF₄-UF₄ (50.46.4 mole %) for 100 hr in seesaw-furnace apparatus with a hot-zone temperature of 1500°F.

8244 brazing alloy (9.4% Cr-4.4% Fe-3.7% Si-2.2% B-0.3% Mn-0.06% C-bol Ni) on Inconel tube-to-header joints showed good corrosion resistance to NaK (56-44 wt %) in a 100-hr seesaw-furnace test at a hot-zone temperature of $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Subsurface voids to a depth of 3 mils were observed on the alloy when tested in NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole %) under similar conditions.

A seesaw-fumace test of 500 hr at a hot-zone temperature of $1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was run to determine the compatibility of a $15\%$ Mo- $6\%$ Cr-bal Ni alloy and molybdenum when exposed to NoF-KF-LiF-UF4 (11.2-41-45.3-2.5 mole $\%$ , fuel 107).
Similar results were found with a molybdenum specimen exposed in a $17\%$ Mo- $2\%$ V-bol Ni alloy capsule to fuel No.
107 under similar conditions.

Thermal cycling did not result in spalling of the $\mathrm{Be}_{2}\mathrm{Cr}$ phase.
A zirconium specimen exposed to sodium for 400 hr at $1600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in an Inconel standpipe capsule was found to have absorbed oxygen from the sodium.
Exposures of 100 and 500 hr at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ resulted in the formation of two extremely hard intermetallic compounds, $\mathsf{MoBe}_2$ and $\mathsf{MoBe}_{33}$ , at the interface.

Four titanium carbide-nickel cermets, three with 10, 20, and $30\%$ nickel and one with $25\%$ Ni plus $5\%$ Mo, were attacked to a depth of approximately 0.5 mil during a 100-hr exposure to sodium at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a seesaw-furnace.
Boron carbide specimens with theoretical densities of 85 and $90\%$ were found to have fair resistance to attack by static sodium in Inconel containers at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
A dense $(2.15\mathrm{g/cm}^3)$ boron nitride specimen was severely attacked in a 100-hr exposure to sodium at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a seesaw furnace.
Exposure of an SiC-Si specimen for 100 hr to sodium at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in a seesaw furnace resulted in complete removal of the silicon.

The corrosion resistance of most of the alloys is equivalent to that of Hostelloy B, and the stress-rupture data for all alloys tested show an average life of 400 hr at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at a stress of 8000 psi.
At present an alloy designated INOR-8, which is based on the Ni-Mo-Cr-Fe system, seems to best fulfill all the requirements.

In order to investigate the range of mechanical properties developed in alloys of this system, the tensile and stress-rupture properties are being determined for a series of compositions, with the maximum content of the major components, other than nickel, being $20\%$ Mo, $10\%$ Cr, and $10\%$ Fe.
The alloys tested to date with low and intermediate contents of Mo, Cr, and Fe are all stronger than Inconel and approach the strength of type 316 stainless steel.
A series of alloys designated INOR-9 are being investigated, in which the elements Cr, Al, Ti, and W have been eliminated because of their poor corrosion resistance in fuel 107.
Alloys with a maximum of $15\%$ Mo and with significant additions of niobium and iron were prepared, and the limits of solubility and oxidation behavior were determined.
These alloys were designed for studies of the effect of $10\%$ Cr and $7\%$ Fe, singly and together, on the corrosion resistance of a $17\%$ Mo-bal Ni base alloy.
A total of 16 tube blanks of four Ni-Mo base alloys which were extruded for Battelle Memorial Institute last quarter failed during tube reduction at Superior Tube Co.
These processing failures substantiate earlier evidence that these alloys with $0.12\%$ C are not amenable to tube-drawing operations.
To demonstrate the feasibility of producing the material, several composite tubes shells of Inconel over type 316 stainless steel were processed on a production schedule.

Nearly identical microstructures were finally prepared from the two materials by an initial anneal at $1450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ followed by severe cold reduction and a long anneal at $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Additional results were obtained in the investigation of the mechanical properties of Hastelloy B and W welds on $\frac{1}{2}$ -in. plate, and the results of all the tensile test data have been analyzed.

Results of tests at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute on experimental and commercial nickel-base alloys indicate that it is usually sufficient to evaluate the hot ductility as measured at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ during cooling from some higher temperature and to compare these data with the hot ductility measured at $2300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ during heating.

The initial result of a study of the effect of brazing and heat-treating operations on the grain growth of Inconel tubing indicates that stress relief treatments at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ do not result in grain growth.
Significant grain growth resulted, however, from $\frac{1}{2}$ -hr brazing cycles at approximately $1922^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Several alloys of this type have exhibited a stress-rupture life of 1000 hr when tested in fuel at 8000 psi and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

text: Welding inspections indicated a performance record of $83\%$ acceptance of critical welds and over $95\%$ acceptance of other standard welds.
Metallographic specimens were cut from in-pile loop No.4 which circulated a fluoride fuel mixture in the MTR.
None of the specimens examined thus far have shown attack greater than 1.5 mils in depth, and no signs of mass transfer have been found.
Colored amorphous deposits were found on the pump slinger and on the forward bellows that were similar to deposits found in in-pile loop No.3.
Duplicate specimens will be tested out-of-pile at stresses of 2000, 3000, and 4000 psi at $1500^{\circ}$ F.

Rupture times of 1050 and 1650 hr were obtained for two specimens of Inconel tubing tested at 2000 psi and $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ while being exposed to a thermal- and fast-neutron flux of $6 \times 10^{12}$ neutrons/cm²·sec in hole HB-3 of the LITR.
These rupture times for this 0.010-in.-wall tubing were in good agreement with out-of-pile data.
A third in-pile specimen, stressed to 1000 psi at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , did not rupture in 1670 hr, and the total creep deformation will be measured upon disassembly of the apparatus.

Examination was completed on a tubular Inconel specimen that was stressed in bending at a maximum stress of 1000 psi and irradiated for 1120 hr at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ while exposed to $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (63-25-12 mole %, fuel 41) on the inside and sodium on the outside.
On the sodium side there was attack to a depth of about 3 mils.
Two MTR-irradiated and two control capsules fabricated of Inconel and filled with fuel mixtures of the NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ system containing 2 mole % UF $_3$ were opened and examined.
The capsule exposed to a flux of 1.5 kw/cm³ for 676 hr at 1500°F and its control capsule showed corrosion penetration so negligible as to be confused with surface faults.
The capsule containing 4 mole % UF $_4$ , which was exposed to a flux of 3.7 kw/cm³, was attacked at the mid-point to a depth of 5 mils in 235 hr at 1500°F.
Irradiation with $\mathrm{Co}^{60}$ gamma radiation and subsequent annealing show a complicated structure in the barrier change of diodes and transistors.

An exposure device has been designed for use in hole 30N of the Graphite Reactor with which a sample can be moved from the external shield to the irradiation position in about 10 sec.
The loss tangents of polyethylene, polystyrene, Teflon, Nylon, and phenol formaldehyde were measured before and after a series of Co 60 irradiations.
All samples showed measurable changes after exposures of $10^{8}$ r; however, the dielectric constant changes of these materials were less than the accuracy of measurement, that is, $\pm 5\%$ .
A nickel foil test was devised which will detect 5 ppm of sulfur in a fused salt.

Under ordinary conditions, however, in equipment with heavy nickel walls, a sulfur contamination of 200 to 500 ppm is necessary to produce any effect in one exposure, but the sulfur effect is cumulative.
An over-all porosity of $65\%$ was found for the type of NaF to be used in the absorption-desorption step of the decontamination process.
The particle and bed porosities were 48 and $33\%$ , respectively.
A critical assembly mocking up the circulating-fuel reflector-moderated reactor under study by Pratt & Whitney Aircraft was operated at temperatures near $1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The fuel consisted of a molten mixture of sodium, zirconium, and uranium fluorides, and the control rod contained a mixture of $30\%$ rare earth oxides and $70\%$ nickel.

With the control rod out, the critical concentration was 10.97 wt $\%$ U (5.1 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ ) at $1258^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; with the rod inserted to the midplane, the critical concentration was 12.20 wt $\%$ U.
The temperature coefficient of reactivity over the range of 1200 to $1350^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was $-3.6 \times 10^{-5} (\Delta k / k)^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

A new instrument positioner has been installed at the Lid Tank Shielding Facility (LTSF) which will indicate the absolute position of any radiation detector in the tank water to within $\pm 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .
This is a considerable improvement over the old positioner with which absolute positions could not be determined to an accuracy greater than $\pm 5\mathrm{mm}$ .

In the pores of the screens (the region in which the flux density is highest) the heat generated will be $2\frac{1}{2}$ times that in the free fluid.

The effect of these regions of high temperature on the overall temperature field is made negligible by two factors: (1) the volume of fluid affected is only a very small percentage of the total fluid volume, and (2) the stringers of high-temperature fluid issuing from the screen pores are rapidly dissipated in the highly turbulent wakes behind the screen wires.

Recent quenching work with mixtures in the LiF-UF $_4$ systems that contain 20 to 32 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ has confirmed the liquidus and solidus relationships previously reported. $^3$ It was found, however, that the compound 4LiF-UF $_4$ undergoes very slow subsolidus decomposition to LiF and 7LiF-6UF $_4$ at about $470^{\circ}$ C.
A series of samples of a mixture containing 28 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , which originally contained no free LiF, was heated overnight and quenched from temperatures both above and below $470^{\circ}$ C.
Just below $470^{\circ}$ C, LiF and 7LiF-6UF $_4$ were found, but, at still lower temperatures, 4LiF-UF $_4$ was found in addition to the other two
Thermal analysis studies showed that liquids containing 20 to 32 mole $\%$ UF4 undercool readily if the melt is first heated to over $800^{\circ}C$ .
Two undercooling halts occur in the cooling curves at about 460 to $480^{\circ}C$ , and the resulting mixtures contain 4LiF.
If the liquid is first heated only to $600^{\circ}C$ , the undercooling effect is absent, and a eutectic type of halt is obtained at about $490^{\circ}C$ .

The solidified mixtures from this type of experiment contain only LiF and 7LiF.6UF4, which are the phases to be expected if 4LiF.6UF4 decomposes during the cooling; in this event the cooling curve should indicate a thermal effect at $470^{\circ}C$ , as well as the eutectic halt at about $490^{\circ}C$ .

Because of the absence of the $470^{\circ}C$ effect and the sluggish nature of the decomposition of 4LiF.6UF4, it did not seem to be likely that the absence of 4LiF.6UF4 could be explained by its decomposition.

These mixtures therefore had apparently cooled to a metastable eutectic between LiF and 7LiF.6UF4, which must freeze only a few degrees below the freezing point at $490^{\circ}C$ of the stable eutectic between 4LiF.6UF4 and 7LiF.6UF4.

In view of the existence of the peculiar thermal effects in the range 20 to 32 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ , a seeding experiment on a mixture containing 24 mole $\%$ UF $_4$ was conducted to be sure that the quenching results previously obtained truly characterize equilibrium conditions.

The mixture was heated to over $800^{\circ}$ C and seeded upon cooling with small amounts of a mixture of the same composition that contained all the phases found in that region [LiF, 4LiF-UF $_4$ , 7LiF-6UF $_4$ , and "3LiF-UF $_4$ " (ref 4)].
The seed material was added at intervals of about $10^{\circ}$ C after the mixture had cooled to about $560^{\circ}$ C.
The cooling curve had halts at 485 and $480^{\circ}$ C, with no evidence of undercooling.
These results are in accordance with the results of quenching studies, except that the temperatures of the thermal effects are about $10^{\circ}$ C lower.

Considerable attention has been given $^{2-4}$ to determining the solubility of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ in $\mathrm{NaF-ZrF}_4$ (53-47 mole $\%$ ).

It was shown previously $^{2}$ that the solubility of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ in this solvent was a function of the total amount of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ added in the range 0.5 to 10 wt $\%$ , but no determinations were made with only traces of the saturating (nickel-containing) phase present.
The results obtained electrochemically $^{3}$ were of considerable interest in this regard, but unfortunately these results appear to be high by approximately a factor of 2.
Consequently, an accurate determination of the solubility at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was undertaken with only traces of the saturating phase present.

In order to approach the saturation concentration gradually, a mixture of $\mathsf{HF}_2$ hydrogen, and helium was bubbled through $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4}$ contained in nickel apparatus at $550^{\circ}C$ .
The filtrates were taken in pairs one at $550^{\circ}C$ and another from the apparatus temporarily heated to $700^{\circ}C$ .

As the solution became saturated, the $550^{\circ}C$ filtrate indicated the solubility of $\mathsf{NiF}_2$ , and the $700^{\circ}C$ filtrate gave a measure of the total amount of $\mathsf{NiF}_2$ in contact with the liquid.
The data obtained for a saturated solution gave a solubility of $352 \pm 11$ ppm Ni as $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ in molten $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF}_4$ (53-47 mole %) at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The data also indicate that the solubility remains constant when the amount of saturating phase is changed from a trace to a total of 1310 ppm $\mathrm{Ni}^{++}$ .
Solubility of $\mathsf{NiF}_2$ in NaF-ZrF4 at $550^{\circ}C$ .
352 ppm is in substantial agreement with a value of 400 ppm previously reported for the solubility obtained with a total addition of 1700 ppm Ni++

Data were reported previously for experiments in which $\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}$ and $\mathsf{Fe}^{\circ}$ were equilibrated with $\mathsf{UF}_4$ ( $\sim 7$ wt $\%$ U) in $\mathsf{RbF - ZrF}_4$ (60-40 mole $\%$ ) as the solvent medium.

these experiments, presented in Table 2.2.3, show that equilibrium is established within 5 hr and that the equilibrium concentration of $\mathsf{Fe}^{+ + }$ is significantly higher at 600 than at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Data for the reaction of $\mathsf{U}\mathsf{F}_4$ with $\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}$ at 600 and $800^{\circ}C$ when $\mathsf{RbF - ZrF_4}$ (52-48 mole $\%$ ) is used as the reaction medium are given in Table 2.2.4.

Approximately $2\textbf{g}$ of $\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}$ was reacted with $\mathsf{U}\mathsf{F}_4$ (8.9 wt $\%$ UF, 4.0 mole $\%$ UF) dissolved in approximately $40\textbf{g}$ of the RbF-ZrF mixture in a nickel container.
The chromium values given in Table 2.2.4 show that the $\mathsf{UF}_4\text{-}\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}$ reaction has a very small temperature coefficient over the temperature range studied.
These equilibrium chromium concentrations are slightly higher than those found in $\mathsf{RbF - ZrF}_4$ (60-40 mole %) at both temperatures.

The small differences indicate that the fluoride ion activity, as measured by the $\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}\text{-}\mathsf{UF}_4$ reaction, increases only slightly when the RbF concentration is increased from 50 to 58 mole %, the RbF concentrations of the ternary mixtures containing 4 mole % $\mathsf{UF}_4$ .

Data were reported previously for the reaction of $\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}$ with $\mathsf{UF}_4$ with low uranium concentrations (0.5 wt $\%$ U) in molten LiF-BeF $_2$ (43-52 mole $\%$ ) as the reaction medium.
These experiments have been extended to include dilute solutions of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ in LiF-ThF $_4$ (72-28 mole $\%$ ) and LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ (67-23-10 mole $\%$ ) mixtures as solvents.
Data for the reaction of UF<sub>4</sub> with Cr<sup>0</sup> at 650 and 800°C in LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub>-ThF<sub>4</sub> (67-23-10 mole %) are given in Table 2.2.5.

Approximately 2 g of Cr<sup>0</sup> was reacted with UF<sub>4</sub> (0.12 mole % UF<sub>4</sub>, 0.5 wt % U) dissolved in approximately 40 g of the LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub>-ThF<sub>4</sub> mixture contained in nickel.
Similar data obtained with LiF-ThF<sub>4</sub> (72-28 mole %) as the reaction medium and a UF<sub>4</sub> concentration of 0.21 mole % (0.5 wt % U) are presented in Table 2.2.6.
The equilibrium chromium values presented in Tables 2.2.5 and 2.2.6 approximate the blank obtained for chromium.

Therefore, the reduction of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ by $\mathsf{Cr}^{\circ}$ in these reaction media when low $\mathsf{UF}_4$ concentrations are used is negligible, as was the case when LiF-BeF $_2$ (48-52 mole %) was used as the reaction medium.

The solubility of $\mathsf{CeF}_3$ in NaF-ZrF4-UF (50-46-4 mole %) was redetermined in three separate experiments with the use of Ce14 as a tracer by the technique described previously.
The solubilities thus obtained, together with those previously reported, are given in Table 2.2.7.
The solubility of $\mathsf{LaF}_3$ in $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (50-46-4 mole %) was redetermined with La<sup>140</sup> as a tracer, and the temperature range of the determinations was extended.
The solubility values thus measured are believed to be the most accurate obtained to data; they are given in Table 2.2.8.
